APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=5] A(5): Yes — the paper tackles dialogue state tracking with a neural, data-driven model (Neural Belief Tracker) using pre-trained semantic vectors, which is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0), compares to appropriate baselines (delexicalisation-based models, Wen et al.), and uses accepted metrics (joint goal accuracy, request accuracy), indicating the right audience and evaluation standards. The problem formulation (per-turn binary classification over slot-value pairs with explicit context modelling) and positioning against prior work that relies on hand-crafted lexicons make it well-aligned with the venue’s research community. | B(5): This paper addresses dialogue state tracking — a core NLP topic — using neural models and pre-trained embeddings, evaluated on standard benchmarks (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0) and compared to relevant baselines (Henderson et al., Wen et al.). The proposed NBT and its context-modelling via gating fit the ML/NLP audience, and the problem formulation, methods (DNN/CNN encoders, end-to-end training, use of Paragram/GloVe), and evaluation metrics (joint goal accuracy, request accuracy) match community expectations. Related work coverage is thorough and limitations are acknowledged, so the paper is positioned correctly within the venue’s research community.

reviewer_a.justification: Yes — the paper tackles dialogue state tracking with a neural, data-driven model (Neural Belief Tracker) using pre-trained semantic vectors, which is squarely within NLP/ML conference scope. It evaluates on standard benchmarks (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0), compares to appropriate baselines (delexicalisation-based models, Wen et al.), and uses accepted metrics (joint goal accuracy, request accuracy), indicating the right audience and evaluation standards. The problem formulation (per-turn binary classification over slot-value pairs with explicit context modelling) and positioning against prior work that relies on hand-crafted lexicons make it well-aligned with the venue’s research community.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper addresses dialogue state tracking — a core NLP topic — using neural models and pre-trained embeddings, evaluated on standard benchmarks (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0) and compared to relevant baselines (Henderson et al., Wen et al.). The proposed NBT and its context-modelling via gating fit the ML/NLP audience, and the problem formulation, methods (DNN/CNN encoders, end-to-end training, use of Paragram/GloVe), and evaluation metrics (joint goal accuracy, request accuracy) match community expectations. Related work coverage is thorough and limitations are acknowledged, so the paper is positioned correctly within the venue’s research community.

SUBSTANCE.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): Experiments are solid: evaluation on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0), comparisons to strong baselines (delexicalisation models with/without semantic dictionaries and Wen et al.), and analyses of different word-vector initialisations (Paragram, GloVe, random) and encoder variants (NBT-DNN, NBT-CNN). Some sensitivity work is present (effect of pre-trained semantic vectors and presence/absence of task-specific lexicons), and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and while some limitations are discussed (vector antonymy issues, weaker gains on WOZ 2.0), more systematic ablations (e.g. isolating the contribution of the gating/context mechanism) and full hyperparameter/sensitivity analyses are lacking. | B(3): The paper evaluates on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) and compares against strong baselines (including delexicalisation with and without semantic dictionaries and Wen et al.), and probes the impact of different pre-trained vectors (Paragram vs GloVe vs random). However, ablation/sensitivity analysis appears limited (no clear ablation of the context-gating mechanism and only two encoder variants), and reproduction is hindered by missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code. Related work is reported as thorough and some limitations (vector shortcomings, weaker gains on WOZ 2.0) are discussed, but the experimental analysis is not comprehensive enough to fully support all methodological claims.

reviewer_a.justification: Experiments are solid: evaluation on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0), comparisons to strong baselines (delexicalisation models with/without semantic dictionaries and Wen et al.), and analyses of different word-vector initialisations (Paragram, GloVe, random) and encoder variants (NBT-DNN, NBT-CNN). Some sensitivity work is present (effect of pre-trained semantic vectors and presence/absence of task-specific lexicons), and related work coverage is described as thorough. However, important reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters not reported, code not mentioned), and while some limitations are discussed (vector antonymy issues, weaker gains on WOZ 2.0), more systematic ablations (e.g. isolating the contribution of the gating/context mechanism) and full hyperparameter/sensitivity analyses are lacking.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper evaluates on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) and compares against strong baselines (including delexicalisation with and without semantic dictionaries and Wen et al.), and probes the impact of different pre-trained vectors (Paragram vs GloVe vs random). However, ablation/sensitivity analysis appears limited (no clear ablation of the context-gating mechanism and only two encoder variants), and reproduction is hindered by missing hyperparameter details and no mention of released code. Related work is reported as thorough and some limitations (vector shortcomings, weaker gains on WOZ 2.0) are discussed, but the experimental analysis is not comprehensive enough to fully support all methodological claims.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper introduces a notable shift from delexicalisation/hand-crafted lexica to an end-to-end, data-driven belief tracker that composes distributed utterance and context representations from pre-trained word vectors — a clear methodological departure from prior SLU-dependent approaches. Its explicit modelling of dialogue context via dot-product gating between candidate slot-value and system-act vectors, plus empirical claims about gains from semantically-specialised Paragram-SL999 vectors, distinguish it from earlier delexicalisation-based and slot-specific parameter methods. Overall this is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated contribution that would inform experts, though it builds on established distributed-representation and classification techniques rather than introducing completely new learning paradigms. | B(4): The paper addresses a well-studied problem (dialogue state tracking) but introduces a notably new method—the Neural Belief Tracker—that departs from delexicalisation by composing distributed utterance and context representations from pre-trained word vectors and applying gating interactions with system-act vectors. It clearly differentiates itself from prior work (e.g. Henderson et al.) by reducing reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and by showing gains from semantically-specialised Paragram-SL999 embeddings. The techniques represent a non-trivial architectural advance rather than a simple combination of existing components, so the contribution is a clear, notable novelty rather than merely incremental.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper introduces a notable shift from delexicalisation/hand-crafted lexica to an end-to-end, data-driven belief tracker that composes distributed utterance and context representations from pre-trained word vectors — a clear methodological departure from prior SLU-dependent approaches. Its explicit modelling of dialogue context via dot-product gating between candidate slot-value and system-act vectors, plus empirical claims about gains from semantically-specialised Paragram-SL999 vectors, distinguish it from earlier delexicalisation-based and slot-specific parameter methods. Overall this is a non-trivial, clearly differentiated contribution that would inform experts, though it builds on established distributed-representation and classification techniques rather than introducing completely new learning paradigms.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper addresses a well-studied problem (dialogue state tracking) but introduces a notably new method—the Neural Belief Tracker—that departs from delexicalisation by composing distributed utterance and context representations from pre-trained word vectors and applying gating interactions with system-act vectors. It clearly differentiates itself from prior work (e.g. Henderson et al.) by reducing reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and by showing gains from semantically-specialised Paragram-SL999 embeddings. The techniques represent a non-trivial architectural advance rather than a simple combination of existing components, so the contribution is a clear, notable novelty rather than merely incremental.

IMPACT.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper addresses an important scalability problem in dialogue state tracking by removing reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and SLU annotations, and demonstrates that the Neural Belief Tracker matches state-of-the-art when lexicons exist and outperforms them otherwise (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0). Its use of pre-trained and semantically-specialised word vectors and explicit modelling of system-act context via gating is likely to change how researchers build data-driven DST models and to be adopted in practice. While not obviously transformative, the approach opens new directions for representation-driven, end-to-end belief tracking applicable across dialogue domains. | B(4): The paper tackles an important scalability and robustness problem in dialogue state tracking by reducing reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and SLU annotations and explicitly modelling context via gating between utterance, candidate slot-value and system-act vectors. Empirically the NBT matches state-of-the-art models that use lexicons and outperforms them when such lexicons are absent, and shows substantial gains using semantically-specialised Paragram-SL999 vectors (notably on noisy DSTC2), which should influence practitioner choices. These contributions open clear follow-on directions (end-to-end, vector-based belief tracking and robust pre-trained embeddings) and are likely to be adopted or built upon across dialogue research, giving the work strong impact though not wholly transformative.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper addresses an important scalability problem in dialogue state tracking by removing reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and SLU annotations, and demonstrates that the Neural Belief Tracker matches state-of-the-art when lexicons exist and outperforms them otherwise (DSTC2, WOZ 2.0). Its use of pre-trained and semantically-specialised word vectors and explicit modelling of system-act context via gating is likely to change how researchers build data-driven DST models and to be adopted in practice. While not obviously transformative, the approach opens new directions for representation-driven, end-to-end belief tracking applicable across dialogue domains.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles an important scalability and robustness problem in dialogue state tracking by reducing reliance on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and SLU annotations and explicitly modelling context via gating between utterance, candidate slot-value and system-act vectors. Empirically the NBT matches state-of-the-art models that use lexicons and outperforms them when such lexicons are absent, and shows substantial gains using semantically-specialised Paragram-SL999 vectors (notably on noisy DSTC2), which should influence practitioner choices. These contributions open clear follow-on directions (end-to-end, vector-based belief tracking and robust pre-trained embeddings) and are likely to be adopted or built upon across dialogue research, giving the work strong impact though not wholly transformative.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression from motivation and main claims through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (NBT framework, context gating, benefits of specialised word vectors). Notation and model descriptions appear consistent and well defined enough to distinguish the NBT-DNN and NBT-CNN variants and the use of pre-trained vectors, and related work, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are thoroughly presented. A minor presentation issue is that experimental hyperparameters are not reported, which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity about training details. | B(4): The paper is well-organized and presents its main contributions clearly early (introducing the NBT, its use of pre-trained vectors, and explicit context modelling). Notation and methodological description appear consistent and focused (NBT-DNN/NBT-CNN, dot-product gating, binary per-slot classifiers), and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related-work coverage. Minor presentation shortcomings include missing details that affect reproducibility and clarity for readers wanting to implement the work (hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear progression from motivation and main claims through method and experiments, and the main contributions are stated early (NBT framework, context gating, benefits of specialised word vectors). Notation and model descriptions appear consistent and well defined enough to distinguish the NBT-DNN and NBT-CNN variants and the use of pre-trained vectors, and related work, datasets, baselines, and evaluation metrics are thoroughly presented. A minor presentation issue is that experimental hyperparameters are not reported, which slightly reduces reproducibility and clarity about training details.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well-organized and presents its main contributions clearly early (introducing the NBT, its use of pre-trained vectors, and explicit context modelling). Notation and methodological description appear consistent and focused (NBT-DNN/NBT-CNN, dot-product gating, binary per-slot classifiers), and the writing quality is reported as good with thorough related-work coverage. Minor presentation shortcomings include missing details that affect reproducibility and clarity for readers wanting to implement the work (hyperparameters are not reported and code availability is not mentioned).

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The main claims are supported by empirical evidence on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) and by comparisons against appropriate baselines (delexicalisation models with/without semantic dictionaries and Wen et al.), showing the NBT matches or outperforms prior work and benefits from semantically-specialised embeddings. The model design (utterance/context encoders, gating via dot-products, end-to-end training with fixed pre-trained vectors) is technically coherent and requires no new unproven mathematics. However, important experimental details are missing from the summary (no hyperparameters reported, no mention of statistical significance or variance), which limits reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims, so these are minor but notable issues. | B(4): The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical comparisons on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 against appropriate baselines (delexicalisation models with/without semantic dictionaries and a recent belief tracker), and the proposed context gating and use of specialised Paragram-SL999 vectors are plausible and shown to improve performance particularly on noisy DSTC2. There are no mathematical derivations to critique and the model description (per-turn binary classifiers over candidate slot–value pairs using fixed pre-trained vectors, NBT-DNN/CNN encoders, cross-entropy + Adam) is consistent. Minor methodological gaps—most notably absence of reported hyperparameters and no mention of statistical significance testing—limit the strength of the empirical claims but do not invalidate the results; no major logical flaws or unsupported assumptions are evident from the provided summary.

reviewer_a.justification: The main claims are supported by empirical evidence on two standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) and by comparisons against appropriate baselines (delexicalisation models with/without semantic dictionaries and Wen et al.), showing the NBT matches or outperforms prior work and benefits from semantically-specialised embeddings. The model design (utterance/context encoders, gating via dot-products, end-to-end training with fixed pre-trained vectors) is technically coherent and requires no new unproven mathematics. However, important experimental details are missing from the summary (no hyperparameters reported, no mention of statistical significance or variance), which limits reproducibility and the strength of empirical claims, so these are minor but notable issues.

reviewer_b.justification: The main claims are clearly stated and appear supported by empirical comparisons on DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 against appropriate baselines (delexicalisation models with/without semantic dictionaries and a recent belief tracker), and the proposed context gating and use of specialised Paragram-SL999 vectors are plausible and shown to improve performance particularly on noisy DSTC2. There are no mathematical derivations to critique and the model description (per-turn binary classifiers over candidate slot–value pairs using fixed pre-trained vectors, NBT-DNN/CNN encoders, cross-entropy + Adam) is consistent. Minor methodological gaps—most notably absence of reported hyperparameters and no mention of statistical significance testing—limit the strength of the empirical claims but do not invalidate the results; no major logical flaws or unsupported assumptions are evident from the provided summary.

MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(5): The paper evaluates against appropriate and recent baselines (Henderson et al. delexicalisation model, a variant with a semantic dictionary, and Wen et al. 2017) on the standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 datasets using the same metrics (joint goal and request accuracy). Comparisons appear fair and conducted under comparable conditions (same data and evaluation metrics), and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (match state-of-the-art with lexicons) and highlight where they outperform (when lexicons are absent). The paper also analyzes differences—highlighting NBT’s use of pretrained semantic vectors and explicit context gating versus hand-crafted lexicons—and the related work coverage is reported as thorough. | B(4): The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (Henderson et al.’s delexicalisation model, a lexicon-augmented baseline, and Wen et al. (2017)) and evaluates on standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) using standard metrics (joint goal and request accuracy). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), the authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (noting weaker gains on the clean WOZ 2.0 data) and they analyze key differences (e.g. explicit context gating and reduced reliance on hand-crafted lexicons). A minor omission is the lack of reported hyperparameters and unspecified code release, which slightly limits reproducibility but does not undermine the comparative claims.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates against appropriate and recent baselines (Henderson et al. delexicalisation model, a variant with a semantic dictionary, and Wen et al. 2017) on the standard DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0 datasets using the same metrics (joint goal and request accuracy). Comparisons appear fair and conducted under comparable conditions (same data and evaluation metrics), and the authors explicitly acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (match state-of-the-art with lexicons) and highlight where they outperform (when lexicons are absent). The paper also analyzes differences—highlighting NBT’s use of pretrained semantic vectors and explicit context gating versus hand-crafted lexicons—and the related work coverage is reported as thorough.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares against appropriate, recent baselines (Henderson et al.’s delexicalisation model, a lexicon-augmented baseline, and Wen et al. (2017)) and evaluates on standard datasets (DSTC2 and WOZ 2.0) using standard metrics (joint goal and request accuracy). Comparisons appear fair (same data and metrics), the authors acknowledge when prior methods perform comparably (noting weaker gains on the clean WOZ 2.0 data) and they analyze key differences (e.g. explicit context gating and reduced reliance on hand-crafted lexicons). A minor omission is the lack of reported hyperparameters and unspecified code release, which slightly limits reproducibility but does not undermine the comparative claims.

main_claims: Introduce the Neural Belief Tracker (NBT), a data-driven belief tracking framework that reasons over pre-trained word vectors to compose distributed representations of user utterances and dialogue context, reducing dependence on hand-crafted lexicons and SLU annotations.
NBT matches performance of state-of-the-art models that rely on hand-crafted semantic lexicons and outperforms them when such lexicons are not provided.

NBT explicitly models dialogue context (notably last system acts: request and confirm) via gating interactions between utterance representations, candidate slot-value pairs, and system-act argument vectors.
Using semantically-specialised Paragram-SL999 word vectors significantly improves goal-tracking performance (especially on noisy DSTC2) compared to GloVe or random initialisation.
_summary.methodology: The NBT builds distributed utterance and context representations from pre-trained word vectors and evaluates, per turn, a binary classifier for each candidate slot-value pair using DNN (NBT-DNN) or CNN (NBT-CNN) encoders, training end-to-end with cross-entropy loss and the Adam optimizer while keeping pre-trained vectors fixed; context is modelled via dot-product gating between candidate and system-act vectors.
baselines: Delexicalisation-based model of Henderson et al. (2014c/d)
Baseline supplemented with task-specific semantic dictionary
Belief tracking model of Wen et al. (2017)
limitations: Delexicalisation-based baseline models require hand-crafted semantic dictionaries, limiting scalability and performance without them (stated as a drawback of prior methods).
Resource requirements of prior methods (learning independent parameters per slot or needing fine-grained manual annotation) hinder scaling to larger domains (stated for related work).
Distributional word vectors (e.g. GloVe) can keep related but antonymous words close (e.g. north/south), reducing useful semantic distinctions.
Gains from pre-trained semantic vectors are weaker on the clean, large WOZ 2.0 dataset compared to noisy DSTC2.
_summary.topic_area: NLP - dialogue state tracking (spoken dialogue systems)
